topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a dropped stone. Subject line: "For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane." My fingers hovered over the screen, a strange, cold knot tightening in my stomach. It was impossible. After fifteen years of careful distance, of moving across continents and building a life somewhere entirely new, this address—the crooked porch swing, the overgrown lilac bush by the back door—was suddenly up for auction. I reread the listing, the photos of the peeling paint and the faded garden gnome making my jaw clench. I leaned back in my ergonomic chair, the squeak of the leather protesting the sudden tension in my shoulders. "No," I muttered to the empty room, my voice sounding thin and brittle. "Absolutely not." I closed the laptop with a sharp snap, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels swam. Another listing. Another ghost from a life I’d meticulously buried under layers of new experiences. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that did nothing to settle the churning in my gut. I paced the length of the living room three times, my heels clicking a frantic rhythm on the hardwood floor. Why now? Why this sudden, unwelcome summons back to the wreckage of my youth? I ran a hand over my face, feeling the flush creep up my neck. "This is a joke," I whispered, the sound laced with a profound weariness. I snatched my phone, already scrolling through property listings, a desperate attempt to drown the initial shock in a sea of new, unrelated possibilities. The frantic swiping only served to amplify the heavy, sinking feeling in my chest.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug went cold before I even registered the notification. I picked up my phone, the glass cool against my palm, and the details of the listing swam into sharp focus. It was the house. My childhood house. The sheer audacity of it made my vision narrow. I dropped the phone onto the counter with a clatter that felt disproportionately loud. I stood by the window, watching the traffic flow below, feeling a profound sense of being cornered. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the sill. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, tasting like ash. I turned away from the view, focusing instead on the mess of papers piled on my desk, wishing with a sudden, intense longing that I could simply erase the digital footprint of this unwelcome memory.
